name: Cold Water Face Immersion
tagline: Cold on the face triggers the body's built-in calming reflex.

origin.source: Mammalian dive reflex research; incorporated into DBT's TIPP skill

situations: Emotion at crisis level, body in overdrive
Panic that won't respond to breathing
On the edge of an impulsive act
Need to physically force the body to calm
summary: Putting cold water on your face — especially around the eyes and cheeks while holding your breath — triggers the mammalian dive reflex, which rapidly slows your heart rate and dampens arousal. It's a fast, physiological way to bring down crisis-level emotion when nothing cognitive is reachable. It's the 'T' (Temperature) in DBT's TIPP skill.
howItWorks: Cold stimulation of the face combined with breath-holding activates the dive reflex, an ancient survival response that conserves oxygen by slowing the heart and shifting the autonomic balance toward the parasympathetic 'rest' system. This produces a quick, involuntary drop in physiological arousal — useful precisely when emotion is too high for any thinking-based skill to work.

steps.instruction: Fill a bowl with cold water (around 10°C / 50°F) or grab an ice pack.
steps.note: Cold but not painful; very cold isn't necessary.

steps.instruction: Take a breath, then bend forward and hold your face in the water — or press the cold pack over your eyes and upper cheeks.
Hold for about 15–30 seconds while keeping still.
Lift, breathe, and repeat once or twice if needed.

whenToUse: Crisis-level emotion that breathing won't touch
When you're close to an impulsive, harmful action
To rapidly drop arousal before using a thinking skill
whenNotToUse: With heart conditions, low heart rate, or on certain heart medications — check with a doctor first
With eating disorders (the cold/bradycardia effect can be risky)
If you have a cold-water phobia that would worsen panic
tips: Target the area around the eyes and cheeks for the strongest reflex
Hold your breath during immersion to deepen the effect
An ice pack over the eyes works when water isn't handy
Use it to get calm enough to then problem-solve

evidence.summary: The mammalian dive reflex and its bradycardic (heart-slowing) effect from cold facial immersion are well documented physiologically; it is incorporated into DBT's evidence-based TIPP skill.
citations: Linehan, M.M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual. Guilford Press.
Foster, G.E. & Sheel, A.W. (2005). The human diving response. Scandinavian J. Med. & Science in Sports.
